title: The first-pass effect
hook: Why a swallowed pill can need ten times the dose of an injection.
body: When you swallow a drug, it is absorbed from your gut and carried straight to the liver by the portal vein before it ever reaches the rest of your body.

The liver can metabolise a large chunk of it on this “first pass” — sometimes 70 to 90 percent. That is why drugs like propranolol or morphine need a much bigger oral dose than an intravenous one to reach the same blood level.
take: Route of administration is not just convenience — it can change the effective dose by an order of magnitude.

quiz.q: Why does an oral dose often need to be larger than an IV dose of the same drug?
options: The stomach acid destroys most of it
The liver metabolises much of it before it reaches circulation
Tablets are less pure than injections
The gut absorbs drugs very slowly
quiz.why: Blood from the gut passes through the liver first, which can clear a large fraction of the dose before it reaches the systemic circulation.

title: Grapefruit vs your medicine cabinet
hook: One breakfast fruit quietly boosts dozens of drugs.
body: Grapefruit contains furanocoumarins that block an enzyme called CYP3A4 in your gut wall — the same enzyme that normally breaks down many drugs before they are absorbed.

With the enzyme knocked out, more drug gets through. For some statins and calcium-channel blockers, a glass of grapefruit juice can raise blood levels enough to matter — and the effect can last a day.
take: A “food” can be a pharmacologically active enzyme inhibitor. Interactions are not only drug-on-drug.

quiz.q: How does grapefruit raise the levels of some drugs?
options: It makes the stomach more acidic
It speeds up the heart so drugs circulate faster
It blocks the gut enzyme (CYP3A4) that breaks them down
It binds the drug and carries it into the blood
quiz.why: Grapefruit inhibits intestinal CYP3A4, so less drug is metabolised before absorption and more reaches the bloodstream.

title: Why meds take days to “kick in”
hook: The magic number is about five.
body: Start a regular dose and the drug builds up until the amount you take each interval equals the amount your body clears. That balance point is called steady state.
It takes roughly four to five half-lives to get there — no matter the dose. A drug with a 24-hour half-life needs about five days before it is doing its full, steady job.
take: Feeling “nothing yet” on day two can be normal pharmacokinetics, not a failed drug.

quiz.q: About how many half-lives does it take to reach steady state?
quiz.why: After ~5 half-lives a drug reaches ~97% of steady state, where intake and elimination balance.

title: How Tylenol can hurt the liver
hook: A safe painkiller with a razor-thin overdose margin.
body: Most acetaminophen is cleared harmlessly, but a small fraction becomes a toxic byproduct called NAPQI. Normally your liver’s glutathione neutralises it instantly.
In overdose, glutathione runs out, NAPQI builds up, and liver cells die. The antidote, N-acetylcysteine, works by restocking glutathione — which is why speed matters.
take: The dose makes the poison: the same molecule is safe and dangerous depending on how much depletes your defenses.

quiz.q: Why is N-acetylcysteine the antidote for acetaminophen overdose?
options: It absorbs the drug in the stomach
It replenishes glutathione so the toxic byproduct is neutralised
quiz.why: NAC restores glutathione, letting the liver detoxify the reactive metabolite NAPQI before it kills liver cells.

title: Narrow therapeutic index
hook: Some drugs live on a knife-edge between “works” and “harm.”
body: For most medicines the helpful dose and the harmful dose are far apart. For a few — warfarin, digoxin, lithium, phenytoin — the gap is tiny.
That is why these get blood-level monitoring, careful brand consistency, and cautious dose changes. A small percentage swing can flip a patient from therapeutic to toxic.
take: The “therapeutic index” — the ratio of toxic to effective dose — is why some drugs are routine and others are watched like hawks.

quiz.q: What defines a “narrow therapeutic index” drug?
options: The effective and toxic doses are very close together
It only comes as an injection
quiz.why: A small margin between the effective and toxic dose demands monitoring — e.g. warfarin, digoxin, lithium.

title: When the body clears a fixed amount, not a fixed fraction
hook: Why the last drink hits harder than the first.
body: Most drugs are cleared by first-order kinetics — a constant fraction per hour. Alcohol and, at higher doses, phenytoin are different: the enzymes saturate and clear a fixed amount per hour instead.
That is zero-order kinetics. Once the system is maxed out, a small extra dose can send levels climbing far more than you would expect.
take: Saturable elimination is why some drugs go from “fine” to “toxic” with a deceptively small dose bump.

quiz.q: In zero-order (saturable) kinetics, the body clears…
options: A constant fraction of drug per hour
A constant amount of drug per hour
quiz.why: When clearing enzymes are saturated, a fixed amount is removed per unit time, so levels can rise sharply with small dose increases.

title: Codeine is a prodrug
hook: It does almost nothing until your genes convert it.
body: Codeine itself is a weak painkiller. The enzyme CYP2D6 converts it into morphine — that is where most of the effect comes from.

People carry different CYP2D6 versions: “poor metabolisers” get little relief, while rare “ultra-rapid metabolisers” make morphine fast, which is why codeine is now avoided in some children and breastfeeding mothers.
take: Genetics can make the same pill a dud for one person and an overdose risk for another — the science of pharmacogenomics.

quiz.q: Why does codeine affect people so differently?
options: Genetic differences in the CYP2D6 enzyme that converts it to morphine
It is a placebo for most people
quiz.why: CYP2D6 activation varies by genotype — poor metabolisers get little effect, ultra-rapid metabolisers form morphine dangerously fast.

title: Left-handed and right-handed drugs
hook: Two molecules, mirror images — one heals, one may not.
body: Many drug molecules come in two mirror-image forms, like a left and right glove. Your receptors are handed too, so often only one form fits and works.
Drug makers sometimes isolate the active hand: esomeprazole is just the “left-handed” half of omeprazole. Same atoms, different geometry, different behaviour.
take: In biology, shape is destiny — mirror-image molecules can have completely different effects.

quiz.q: Why can two mirror-image forms of a drug behave differently?
options: Receptors are shape-specific, so often only one form fits
quiz.why: Enantiomers share atoms but differ in 3-D shape; since receptors are chiral, they often bind only one form.

title: The Rule of 72
hook: Double your money in your head.
body: Want to know how long an investment takes to double? Divide 72 by the annual return. At 7 percent, about ten years. At 9 percent, about eight.
It is a shortcut for exponential growth accurate enough for mental math — and a quick reality check on any “too good” return.
take: Compounding is exponential, and 72 is the pocket calculator for it.

quiz.q: At an 8% annual return, roughly how long to double your money?
quiz.why: 72 ÷ 8 = 9 years. The Rule of 72 estimates doubling time from the growth rate.

title: One ticker, thousands of companies
hook: What you actually own when you buy XEQT.
body: An all-equity index fund like XEQT holds a slice of thousands of companies across Canada, the US, and the world — automatically weighted and rebalanced.
Instead of betting on a single winner, you own the whole market’s growth. Diversification is the rare “free lunch”: it lowers risk without necessarily lowering expected return.
take: Index investing turns “pick the winner” into “own them all” — and history has been kind to the whole market.

quiz.q: What is the main benefit of a broad index fund?
options: Diversification — spreading risk across many holdings
It beats the market every year
quiz.why: Owning thousands of companies spreads out single-company risk while capturing the market’s overall return.

title: TFSA vs RRSP, the tax twist
hook: They can be mathematically identical — until your tax rate changes.
body: An RRSP gives you a deduction now and is taxed when you withdraw. A TFSA is funded with after-tax money but grows and comes out tax-free.
If your tax rate is the same now and in retirement, they end up equal. The real edge comes from the difference: contribute to the RRSP when your rate is high, lean on the TFSA when it is low.
take: The best account depends on your tax rate today versus in retirement — not on which “feels” tax-free.

quiz.q: When does an RRSP tend to beat a TFSA?
options: When your tax rate is higher now than in retirement
When your tax rate is lower now than later
quiz.why: You deduct at your high current rate and withdraw at a lower future rate, keeping the difference.

title: The quiet cost of fees
hook: A 2% fee can eat a third of your retirement.
body: Fund fees look tiny — one or two percent a year — but they compound against you for decades. Over 30 years, paying 2 percent instead of 0.2 percent can cost you a third or more of your ending wealth.
That is why low-cost index funds (XEQT’s fee is around 0.2 percent) quietly beat most expensive, actively managed ones.
take: Fees compound just like returns — small percentages become enormous sums over a lifetime.

quiz.q: Why do small annual fees matter so much?
options: They compound against you for decades
quiz.why: A fee drag compounds every year, so over decades even ~2% can erase a large share of your final balance.

title: Dollar-cost averaging
hook: Boring, automatic, and hard to beat.
body: Investing a fixed amount on a schedule means you buy more shares when prices are low and fewer when they are high — automatically.
It will not always beat lump-sum investing, but it removes the impossible job of timing the market and the emotion that wrecks most portfolios.
take: Automation beats willpower: a steady schedule sidesteps both bad timing and panic.

quiz.q: What does dollar-cost averaging protect you from most?
options: The need to time the market perfectly
quiz.why: Buying on a fixed schedule spreads out your entry price and removes market-timing pressure.

title: Real vs nominal returns
hook: A 7% gain in a 3% inflation year is really 4%.
body: Nominal return is the number on the statement. Real return subtracts inflation — the loss of purchasing power.
Money in a “safe” account earning 2 percent while prices rise 3 percent is quietly shrinking in real terms. Inflation is the tax nobody sends you a bill for.
take: Always ask “return after inflation?” — real return is what actually grows your buying power.

quiz.q: If an investment returns 6% while inflation is 3%, the real return is about…
quiz.why: Real return ≈ nominal minus inflation, so 6% − 3% ≈ 3% of true purchasing-power growth.

title: How your Tesla battery works
hook: Charging is just moving lithium ions to one side.
body: A lithium-ion cell shuttles lithium ions between two electrodes through a liquid electrolyte. Charging pushes them into the graphite anode; using power lets them flow back, releasing energy.
No metal is “burned” — it is a reversible dance of ions, which is why the same cell can cycle hundreds of times.
take: A battery does not store electricity — it stores a chemical arrangement that can release it on demand.

quiz.q: What actually moves inside a lithium-ion battery as it charges?
quiz.why: Charging drives lithium ions into the anode; discharging lets them return, moving energy in and out reversibly.

title: Why EV range drops in the cold
hook: Your BC winter costs you kilometres.
body: Cold slows the chemical reactions inside the battery, so it delivers less energy — and heating the cabin draws power straight from the pack instead of from waste engine heat.
Together these can cut range 20 to 40 percent on a frosty morning. Preconditioning while plugged in warms the battery using grid power, not your range.
take: EV range is chemistry plus climate — and warming the pack before you unplug is nearly free.

quiz.q: Why does an EV lose range in the cold?
options: Slower battery chemistry plus cabin heating draw from the pack
quiz.why: Cold reduces the battery’s deliverable energy, and cabin heat comes from the pack — unlike a gas car’s waste heat.

title: Regenerative braking
hook: Your motor moonlights as a generator.
body: When you lift off the accelerator, the electric motor runs backwards as a generator, turning the car’s motion back into electricity and feeding it to the battery.
That is why EVs use “one-pedal driving” and why their brake pads can last far longer than a gas car’s.
take: Braking normally throws away energy as heat; regen recaptures a chunk of it instead.

quiz.q: How does regenerative braking recharge the battery?
options: The motor acts as a generator, converting motion to electricity
It does not — that is a myth
quiz.why: Running the motor as a generator turns kinetic energy back into stored electrical energy.

title: How a heat pump beats 100% efficient
hook: Your Tesla’s heater cheats — legally.
body: A resistive heater turns 1 unit of electricity into 1 unit of heat. A heat pump instead moves existing heat from outside to inside, delivering 2 to 4 units of warmth per unit of electricity.
It is not breaking physics — it is pumping heat, not making it. That “coefficient of performance” above 1 is why newer Teslas and home heating are switching to them.
take: Moving energy is far cheaper than creating it — the core trick behind modern efficient heating.

quiz.q: How can a heat pump deliver more heat energy than the electricity it uses?
options: It moves existing heat rather than generating it
quiz.why: A heat pump transports ambient heat indoors, so output heat can be several times the electrical input.

title: What an AI is really doing
hook: It is an extremely good next-word guesser.
body: A large language model turns your words into numbers, then predicts the most likely next chunk of text, over and over, one piece at a time.
It has no database it looks things up in — the “knowledge” is baked into billions of tuned weights. Fluent reasoning emerges from prediction at massive scale.
take: Today’s AI is pattern prediction taken to an extreme — powerful, but worth double-checking on facts.

quiz.q: At its core, a language model is…
options: Predicting the next piece of text one step at a time
quiz.why: It generates text by repeatedly predicting the most probable next token from learned weights.

title: GPS runs on Einstein
hook: Your location would drift 10 km a day without relativity.
body: GPS satellites carry atomic clocks. Their speed makes those clocks tick slightly slower (special relativity), while weaker gravity up high makes them tick faster (general relativity) — the second effect wins.
Net, they gain about 38 microseconds a day. Uncorrected, that error would push your position off by kilometres within hours. The fix is built in.
take: A 1915 theory about space and time is quietly running inside the phone in your pocket.

quiz.q: Why must GPS account for relativity?
options: Satellites move too fast to track
Time runs differently for the satellites, and uncorrected errors compound into kilometres
The Earth’s magnetic field bends the signal
quiz.why: Special and general relativity shift the satellite clocks ~38 µs/day; uncorrected, positions would drift kilometres daily.

title: The padlock that anyone can close
hook: How strangers share secrets safely.
body: Public-key cryptography gives you two keys: a public one you hand out freely, and a private one you never share. Anything locked with your public key can only be opened by your private key.
So anyone can send you a secret by “snapping shut” your public padlock — but only you can open it. This is what secures the little lock in your browser.
take: You can publish a lock to the whole world and still be the only one who can open it — the foundation of online trust.

quiz.q: In public-key cryptography, what can the public key do?
options: Encrypt a message that only the private key can decrypt
quiz.why: The public key locks messages; only the matching private key can unlock them, enabling secure exchange with strangers.

title: Why the sky is blue
hook: And why sunsets are red — same reason.
body: Air molecules scatter short blue wavelengths far more than long red ones. Overhead, that scattered blue light reaches your eyes from every direction, painting the sky.
At sunset, light travels through much more atmosphere, so the blue is scattered away entirely and the reds and oranges survive the trip.
take: Colour in the sky is not pigment — it is physics sorting light by wavelength.

quiz.q: Why is the daytime sky blue?
options: Air scatters short blue wavelengths more than red
The sun emits mostly blue light
quiz.why: Rayleigh scattering favours short wavelengths, so blue light is scattered across the sky.

title: What caffeine actually blocks
hook: It does not add energy — it hides tiredness.
body: As you stay awake, a molecule called adenosine builds up and makes you sleepy by binding its receptors. Caffeine is shaped just enough like adenosine to sit in those receptors without activating them.
So caffeine does not power you up — it blocks the “you are tired” signal. When it wears off, the backed-up adenosine floods in, and the crash hits.
take: Caffeine is a receptor blocker — a tidy example of how shape lets one molecule impersonate another.

quiz.q: How does caffeine keep you alert?
options: It blocks adenosine receptors so you do not feel sleepy
quiz.why: Caffeine competitively blocks adenosine receptors, masking the sleepiness signal rather than creating energy.

title: Why ice floats
hook: A quirk of water that keeps lakes alive.
body: Almost every substance gets denser when it freezes. Water is a rebel: its molecules lock into an open, hexagonal lattice that takes up more space, so ice is about 9 percent less dense than liquid water.
That is why ice floats — and why lakes freeze top-down, leaving fish a liquid world underneath all winter.
take: One odd property of water — solid being lighter than liquid — makes life in cold climates possible.

quiz.q: Why does ice float on water?
options: Frozen water forms an open lattice that is less dense than liquid
quiz.why: Water expands into a hexagonal lattice when it freezes, making ice ~9% less dense than liquid water.

title: mRNA vaccines are software for cells
hook: Pharmacy meets programming.
body: An mRNA vaccine is a set of instructions — code — telling your own cells to build one harmless piece of a virus so your immune system can learn it.

The cell’s ribosomes are the “hardware” that run the code; then the mRNA degrades within days, leaving no lasting change. Swap the instructions and you have a new vaccine — the platform stays the same.
take: Treating biology like a programmable platform is why mRNA shots could be designed in days, not years.

quiz.q: What does the mRNA in a vaccine actually do?
options: It instructs your cells to build a harmless viral piece to train immunity
quiz.why: mRNA is a temporary instruction; ribosomes build a viral antigen, the immune system learns it, then the mRNA breaks down.

title: Your meds and your savings share a curve
hook: Pharmacology meets compound interest.
body: Take a drug repeatedly and it climbs toward a plateau as intake balances elimination — that is steady state. Invest repeatedly and your balance climbs as contributions compound.
Both are governed by the same exponential mathematics of repeated inputs against a rate. Understand one and you already understand the other.
take: The same equation quietly runs your bloodstream and your brokerage account — exponential accumulation.

quiz.q: What do drug steady-state and compound investing have in common?
options: Both follow exponential accumulation from repeated inputs
quiz.why: Repeated dosing and repeated investing are both exponential processes of inputs accumulating against a rate.

title: Why new drugs cost so much
hook: Pharmacy meets economics.
body: The pill is cheap to make; the knowledge is not. A new drug can cost over a billion dollars once you count the many failures for each success, and patents give ~20 years to recoup it.
When the patent expires, generic makers copy the recipe and prices can fall 80 percent or more — the “patent cliff.” You are mostly paying for the R&D bet, not the powder.
take: Drug prices are an economics story about sunk research costs and time-limited monopolies, not just chemistry.

quiz.q: Why do generic drugs cost so much less than the brand?
options: They skip the huge R&D cost the original had to recoup
quiz.why: Generics copy a proven recipe after patent expiry, avoiding the original’s enormous research and failure costs.

title: Battery aging is a stability curve
hook: Tech meets pharmacy shelf-life.
body: A pharmacist watches drugs degrade slowly by chemical reactions — faster in heat, tracked as an expiry date. A battery ages the same way: slow side-reactions from calendar time plus wear from each charge cycle.
Both are minimised by the same trick — keep them cool and avoid extremes. Storing a battery at 100 percent in the heat is like leaving medicine on a hot dashboard.
take: Degradation is chemistry over time; heat is the universal accelerant, whether it is a pill or a battery.

quiz.q: What most accelerates both drug degradation and battery aging?
quiz.why: Higher temperature speeds the chemical reactions behind both drug breakdown and battery capacity loss.

title: A drug drip is a thermostat
hook: Pharmacy meets control engineering.
body: Titrating a medication — nudging the dose up or down toward a target effect — is exactly what a thermostat does with temperature: measure, compare to a setpoint, adjust, repeat.
This feedback loop shows up everywhere: insulin pumps, cruise control, even how your body holds its own temperature. Same idea, different setpoint.
take: Feedback control is one idea wearing many coats — from an IV drip to your car’s cruise control.

quiz.q: What do titrating a drug and a thermostat share?
options: Both are feedback loops: measure, compare to a target, adjust
quiz.why: Both continually measure a value, compare it to a setpoint, and adjust to close the gap — classic feedback control.

title: Antibiotic resistance is evolution, sped up
hook: Pharmacy meets natural selection.
body: Every antibiotic course is a selection pressure: the susceptible bacteria die, and any rare survivor with a resistance trait now has the field to itself and multiplies.

Because bacteria reproduce in minutes and swap resistance genes like trading cards, this is evolution you can watch in real time — and why using antibiotics only when needed protects the whole toolkit.
take: Overusing antibiotics breeds the very resistance that makes them fail — Darwin on fast-forward.

quiz.q: Why does antibiotic overuse drive resistance?
options: It selects for rare resistant bacteria that then multiply
It weakens the immune system permanently
Bacteria learn to like the drug
quiz.why: Antibiotics kill susceptible bacteria, leaving resistant survivors to reproduce — natural selection in fast motion.

title: The blood-brain barrier
hook: Why most drugs can never reach the brain.
body: The brain is guarded by a wall of tightly-joined cells that blocks most molecules from crossing out of the blood. It keeps toxins out — but it also keeps most drugs out.
Only small, fat-soluble molecules slip through easily. That is why treating brain conditions is so hard, and why a drug that works everywhere else may do nothing in the brain.
take: Getting a drug to the right place can be harder than designing the drug itself.

quiz.q: Why do many drugs fail to treat brain conditions?
options: The blood-brain barrier blocks them from entering
The brain has no blood supply
quiz.why: The blood-brain barrier admits mostly small, fat-soluble molecules, keeping most drugs out of brain tissue.

title: Why some pills hate milk
hook: Calcium can handcuff an antibiotic.
body: Antibiotics like tetracyclines and some fluoroquinolones bind tightly to metals such as calcium, magnesium and iron — the very minerals in milk, antacids and supplements.
The drug and the metal clump together into something your gut cannot absorb, so the antibiotic never reaches the bloodstream. Spacing the doses by a couple of hours fixes it.
take: A harmless glass of milk can quietly cancel a dose — timing and food matter as much as the pill.

quiz.q: Why should some antibiotics be separated from dairy?
options: Calcium binds the drug so it cannot be absorbed
Milk speeds the drug up too much
quiz.why: Calcium and other metals chelate the antibiotic into an unabsorbable complex, cutting how much drug gets in.

title: Why cramming fails
hook: Your brain learns on a schedule, not in a marathon.
body: Studying the same material spread across days — the spacing effect — builds far stronger memories than the same hours crammed at once. Each time you almost forget and then recall, the memory strengthens.
It is why flashcard apps show you a card right before you would forget it. Curio uses the same idea: a little, often.
take: Forgetting a bit and recalling is not failure — it is exactly how durable memory is built.

quiz.q: What makes memories stick best?
options: Studying spread out over time with recall
quiz.why: The spacing effect: distributed practice with retrieval produces much stronger long-term memory than massed cramming.

title: Losses hurt twice as much
hook: Why you would fight harder to keep $100 than to win it.
body: Experiments show a loss feels roughly twice as painful as the pleasure of an equal gain. This loss aversion nudges us to hold losing investments too long and to fear missing out.
Knowing the bias is half the defense: it lets you notice when fear, not logic, is steering a decision.
take: Our brains are wired to dread losses more than they enjoy gains — great for survival, tricky for investing.

quiz.q: What does “loss aversion” describe?
options: Losses feel about twice as strong as equal gains
quiz.why: People weigh a loss roughly twice as heavily as an equivalent gain, biasing many financial choices.

title: The first number wins
hook: Why a “was $200” price tag works.
body: Show someone any number before they estimate a value and their guess drifts toward it — even a random one. This is anchoring, and it quietly shapes negotiations, pricing and tips.
The original “$200” on a sale tag exists to anchor you high, so “$120” feels like a steal.
take: The first figure on the table pulls every later number toward it — set anchors, and notice when one is set on you.

quiz.q: The anchoring effect means…
options: An initial number biases later estimates
quiz.why: An initial reference value “anchors” judgment, pulling subsequent estimates toward it.

title: Why phone numbers have dashes
hook: Your working memory holds about four things.
body: Short-term memory can juggle only around four chunks at once. But a chunk can be big: “CBC” is one chunk, not three letters.
Grouping digits — like 604-555-1234 — turns ten hard items into three easy chunks. Expertise is largely learning to chunk bigger.
take: You do not get more memory slots — you get better at packing each one.

quiz.q: Roughly how many chunks can working memory hold at once?
quiz.why: Modern estimates put working-memory capacity near four chunks — but chunking lets each hold more.

title: Why the Moon shows one face
hook: We have never seen its far side from Earth.
body: The Moon spins on its axis in exactly the time it takes to orbit Earth — so the same side always faces us. This “tidal locking” came from Earth’s gravity gently braking the Moon’s spin over eons.
The hidden far side is not dark — it gets just as much sun. It is simply never pointed our way.
take: Gravity can sync a moon’s spin to its orbit, hiding half a world in plain sight.

quiz.q: Why do we always see the same side of the Moon?
options: Its rotation is tidally locked to its orbit
The far side is always dark
quiz.why: Tidal locking makes the Moon rotate once per orbit, keeping one face toward Earth.

title: A spoonful weighing a billion tonnes
hook: The densest thing you can name.
body: When a massive star collapses, its core can be crushed until protons and electrons merge into neutrons packed shoulder to shoulder. The result is a neutron star: a city-sized ball as heavy as the Sun.
A single teaspoon of it would weigh about a billion tonnes — matter squeezed to almost unimaginable density.
take: Gravity, pushed to its limit, can crush a whole star into a sphere you could drive across.

quiz.q: What is a neutron star made of?
quiz.why: Stellar collapse packs matter into neutrons so densely that a teaspoon would weigh around a billion tonnes.

title: The sunlight you see is old
hook: Look up: that light left the Sun 8 minutes ago.
body: Light is fast but not instant. It takes about eight minutes to cross the 150 million kilometres from the Sun to your eyes.
So you never see the Sun as it is now — only as it was eight minutes ago. Look further out and you are looking further back in time; some stars you see may no longer exist.
take: To look into space is to look into the past — distance is a time machine.

quiz.q: How long does sunlight take to reach Earth?
quiz.why: At light speed, the ~150-million-km trip from Sun to Earth takes roughly eight minutes.

title: We cannot see 95% of the universe
hook: Everything you know is the other 5%.
body: Add up all the stars, planets and gas and you get only about 5 percent of the universe. The rest is dark matter, which we detect only by its gravity, and dark energy, which is pushing space apart.
We have never directly seen either. The cosmos is mostly made of things we cannot yet explain.
take: The most honest fact in science: most of reality is still a mystery.

quiz.q: About how much of the universe is ordinary visible matter?
quiz.why: Ordinary matter is ~5%; the rest is dark matter (~27%) and dark energy (~68%), neither directly seen.

title: Why February gets an extra day
hook: The calendar is patching a rounding error.
body: Earth takes about 365.25 days to circle the Sun, not a tidy 365. Ignore that quarter-day and the seasons slowly drift out of the calendar.
A leap day every four years fixes most of it — with a clever tweak skipping some century years to stay precise. It is a rounding error we correct one day at a time.
take: Our calendar is an ongoing negotiation between human counting and the messy real orbit.

quiz.q: Why do we add a leap day?
options: Earth’s orbit is about 365.25 days, so we catch up the extra quarter-days
quiz.why: The ~0.25 extra day per year accumulates; a leap day every four years keeps the calendar aligned with the seasons.

title: The 2,000-year-old computer
hook: The Greeks built a gear-driven calculator.
body: Divers found a corroded lump of bronze in a Roman-era shipwreck. It turned out to be the Antikythera mechanism: dozens of precise gears that predicted eclipses and planetary positions.
Nothing else this complex appears for another thousand years. It hints that ancient know-how could be lost as easily as it was found.
take: Progress is not a straight line — knowledge can be invented, forgotten, and rediscovered.

quiz.q: What was the Antikythera mechanism?
options: An ancient geared device that predicted astronomical events
quiz.why: It is a ~2,000-year-old Greek analog computer of bronze gears that modelled the heavens.

title: Why time zones exist
hook: Blame the railways.
body: For most of history every town set noon by its own Sun, so clocks differed town to town. That was fine until trains made schedules impossible across dozens of local times.
Railways pushed for standardized zones in the 1800s, and the world was carved into hourly slices. Your clock is really a shared agreement, not a fact of nature.
take: The time on your wall is a human convention, invented to make trains run on time.

quiz.q: What drove the creation of standard time zones?
quiz.why: Rail travel made town-by-town local time unworkable, prompting standardized hourly zones.

title: Bigger than Central Park
hook: A hometown fact for the drive.
body: Vancouver’s Stanley Park is about 400 hectares — roughly 20 percent larger than New York’s Central Park, and it is ringed by a seawall you can walk or bike almost 9 kilometres around.
Most of it is near-natural rainforest, with some trees centuries old, sitting minutes from downtown.
take: One of the continent’s great urban parks is right in your backyard — and it beats Central Park on size.

quiz.q: How does Stanley Park compare to Central Park?
quiz.why: Stanley Park is ~400 ha versus Central Park’s ~341 ha — about a fifth larger.

title: Why you can’t unscramble an egg
hook: The one law time obeys.
body: The second law of thermodynamics says disorder — entropy — tends to increase. There are countless messy arrangements of a broken egg and only a few tidy ones, so nature drifts toward mess.
That statistical one-way street is why heat flows hot to cold, and arguably why time seems to run forward at all.
take: The arrow of time is really just probability — order is rare, disorder is easy.

quiz.q: What does the second law of thermodynamics say tends to happen?
quiz.why: Entropy statistically increases because disordered states vastly outnumber ordered ones.

title: Silence made of sound
hook: How headphones cancel a jet engine.
body: Noise-cancelling headphones listen to the roar around you, then play a sound wave that is its exact mirror image. Where a peak meets a trough, they cancel to near silence.
It is not muffling — it is fighting sound with equal and opposite sound, computed in real time.
take: Two waves can add up to nothing — destructive interference turned into everyday quiet.

quiz.q: How do noise-cancelling headphones work?
options: They play an inverted wave that cancels the noise
quiz.why: They generate an anti-phase wave; peak-meets-trough interference cancels the incoming noise.

title: Your WiFi and microwave are cousins
hook: They work at the same frequency.
body: Both older WiFi and your microwave oven use radio waves around 2.4 gigahertz. The oven blasts high power to shake water molecules and heat food; WiFi whispers at tiny power to carry data.
It is why a running microwave can sometimes stutter a weak WiFi signal — same neighbourhood of the spectrum, wildly different volume.
take: Same radio frequency, a million-fold difference in power — one cooks, one connects.

quiz.q: What do WiFi and microwave ovens share?
quiz.why: Both operate near 2.4 GHz; the oven uses enormous power to heat, WiFi uses tiny power to transmit data.

title: Carbon dating is a half-life clock
hook: Science meets pharmacology’s favourite number.
body: The same math that clears a drug from your blood dates ancient bones. Radioactive carbon-14 decays with a fixed half-life of about 5,700 years, just as a drug falls by half each half-life.
Measure how much is left and you can read the clock — whether it is a dose in the bloodstream or a bone in the ground.
take: One idea — exponential decay by half-lives — measures both a medicine and a mummy.

quiz.q: What links carbon dating and drug clearance?
options: Both follow exponential decay by half-lives
quiz.why: Radioactive decay and first-order drug elimination both halve a quantity every half-life.
